title: Religious Trauma and the Rage at Heaven
dek: Your anger at God isn't blasphemy. It's a biological truth your body has held since the first time you were told to be meek. The body remembers the hands that folded you into submission, the throat that swallowed screams during hymns. This is not a test of faith—it's a nervous system finally completing a defensive response stolen decades ago. Here's how to let it finish.
bodyMarkdown:
What would you do with your rage if you were certain that no one was watching—not your former pastor, not your mother, not the version of God who supposedly counts every hair on your head?

You might stop holding your breath. You might unclench the jaw that lockstight whenever someone says “God’s will.” You might finally let your middle finger rise toward the ceiling of your childhood bedroom, the one with the glow-in-the-dark stars you stuck there because you were told Heaven was just beyond them. That gesture—the one you’ve rehearsed only in the privacy of your locked car—isn’t a sin. It’s your diaphragm finally moving again.

## The Body Still Kneels

You left the building years ago. The pews, the incense or the cheap grape juice, the man at the microphone who made you stand and recite that you deserved eternal torture—you walked away. But your body didn’t get the memo. It still drops into a submissive posture the moment you feel watched. Shoulders rolled forward, chin dipped, breath shallow and held high in the chest like a trapped bird. Pat Ogden, founder of sensorimotor psychotherapy, mapped how the body’s procedural memory encodes these postures of submission long after the mind rejects the creed. Your body learned, before you had words, that a curved spine might deflect a blow—or a divine gaze. That collapse became a chronic shape. It’s not weakness; it’s an enduring, somatic prayer to a god you no longer believe in.

Notice it now. Read this sentence and scan your body. Where are your shoulders? Are your thighs pressed together? Is there a slight clench in your buttocks, a tucking of your tailbone that you associate with “being good”? That pose is the residue of a liturgical childhood: sit still, don’t wiggle, fold your hands, bow your head. Your nervous system interpreted stillness as safety because movement brought punishment. Decades later, stillness still triggers a neuroceptive signal of threat—your body braces for the smite every time you try to rest. The body remembers submission more faithfully than your mind ever will.

## The Anger You Were Forbidden Is the Door, Not the Sin

You were taught that anger is a deadly sin, second only to pride in its ability to send you downward. Wrath. The word itself tastes metallic, like old pennies. But Karla McLaren, who spent years mapping the emotional realm, insists that anger has only one job: to restore your boundaries. It flares when something unconscionable has trespassed into your territory—when a parent’s belt, a pastor’s hand, or a deity’s silence has crossed a line that your soul required to remain sacred. The anger is not proof of your depravity. It’s the alarm system for your violated autonomy.

But in religious space, that alarm was rewired. You learned to turn your anger inward, to call it ingratitude or rebellion. You learned to apologize for the very sensation that was trying to save you. The counterintuitive truth is this: your rage at Heaven is not a lapse of faith. It is the first honest prayer you’ve ever prayed. It’s your body saying, “That thing that happened under the guise of love—it was wrong, and I will no longer pretend it wasn’t.” If you can stop moralizing the anger, you might find that it leads exactly to the place you were told only grace could reach: the truth of what was done to you.

## When Your Nervous System Prays Without You

Peter Levine, who built Somatic Experiencing from the ground up, demonstrated that trauma is not stored in the story you tell about the event. It lives in the incomplete, thwarted survival responses your body couldn’t finish—the punch you didn’t throw, the scream you didn’t release, the flight your legs were too terrified to take. In a religious setting, these incompletions pile up like kindling. How many times were you made to sit still while an adult violated your sense of self—sometimes physically, more often emotionally—and told it was holy? Your nervous system logged every instance as a failed escape. Now, decades later, it keeps trying to complete the action. The sudden surge of heat in your chest when someone mentions “sovereignty” or “submission” is not a character flaw. It’s your body mobilizing a fight response that was banned at the altar.

Feel that heat. Let it travel to your hands. Your palms might prickle, your fingers twitch. That’s the motor cortex revving an engine that has been idling since you were seven and the Sunday school teacher told you your questions made Jesus sad. This is not a crisis you need to pray away. It’s biology, and it wants to move.

## The Counterfeit of Forgiveness

You’ve been told forgiveness is the only path to peace. That you must release the debt, turn the other cheek, bless those who persecuted you—often before you’ve even acknowledged that persecution occurred. This is not forgiveness; it’s spiritual bypassing dressed in a white robe. Real forgiveness, if it ever comes, is a byproduct of a nervous system that has discharged its righteous fury. It cannot be forced by a Bible verse quoted at you by someone who is uncomfortable with your pain.

Consider what happens in your gut when you hear “forgive and forget.” For many, the solar plexus knots, the stomach acid surges. Some people find that [Banyan Botanicals Pitta Digest (90 tablets)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B075HV67JJ?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a physiological softening when the internal fire becomes literal heartburn—the body’s frank language for swallowed rage. But herbs only support; they don’t replace the need to spit out what you were forced to eat. Your inability to forgive is not a spiritual failure. It’s an instinctive refusal to whitewash a crime that still lives in your cells. Until the anger has been felt—fully, somatically, without condemnation—forgiveness is just another cage.

## The Practice: A Five-Minute Table for Your Rage

This is not a journaling prompt. It’s a body practice. You’ll need a table or a hard surface you can press against, and a little privacy.

Sit at the table. Place both palms flat on the surface, shoulder-width apart. Feel the temperature of the wood or laminate against your skin. Now slowly push down, as if you’re trying to depress the entire structure through the floor. Push until your arms tremble. Let your jaw unhinge while you do this—mouth slightly open, teeth apart. Many of us clamp our molars as a learned way to hold back sound. Release it. You might hear a low sound escape your throat. A groan, a sigh, a half-formed growl. Let it.

Now, still pushing, imagine the table is the immovable force of everything you were told about a god who watched you suffer in silence. Push harder. If words rise, say them aloud. You can hiss “no.” You can say “fuck you.” The ceiling won’t open up and damn you. I promise. This push activates your ventral vagal system’s mobilization response—a safe way to complete the fight impulse. Do this for two minutes, then release abruptly. Let your hands fall to your lap. Sit with the vibration, the heat in your shoulders, the blood returning to your fingers. The body remembers safety, too. It just needs a physical argument to override the old edicts.

## Why Your Throat Closes When You Try to Speak

The hymnal in your childhood church might have claimed that “He is exalted,” but your throat learned a different song: the clamp of silence. For many survivors of religious trauma, the larynx becomes a battleground. You try to voice the violation, and the words stick like dry bread. That’s not a demon. It’s a hyperactive vagus nerve branch that innervates the vocal cords, conditioned by years of repressing truth. The body treats honest speech as a threat because, once, it was.

Pat Ogden noted that the “procedural memory” of freezing in the face of an overwhelming authority figure can manifest as a chronic constriction in the throat. You might have been told you had a “spirit of fear.” You didn’t. You had a completely rational physiological response to being silenced by people who claimed to speak for the Almighty. When you now try to talk back to that Almighty, your throat seizes because your nervous system cannot distinguish between a silent sky and a looming pulpit. [Gaia Herbs Skullcap (60 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0009F3SB4?tag=spankyspinola-20) is one nervous-system ally that some people use when the constriction feels unbearable—a nervine that can soften the edge without dulling the necessary anger. But the lasting release comes from vibrating sound through that throat: humming, wailing, chanting a curse word you were never allowed to say. Your vocal cords need to learn they are allowed to take up space.

## The Loneliness of Holy Rage

You’ll face a peculiar isolation: the secular world often doesn’t get it, and the religious world condemns it. Your therapist might nod sympathetically at your “spiritual abuse,” then shift in their chair when you describe the hot, specific hatred you still carry for a God you’re not sure you believe in. Friends from healthy families don’t understand why you can’t just “let it go.” And the faithful in your past will diagnose you with demonic oppression before they’ll consider that your rage is a rational response to divinely sanctioned harm. This loneliness can feel like proof that you really are damned—a circular logic that kept you trapped for decades.

But the anger doesn’t need an audience. It doesn’t need validation from anyone who hasn’t lain awake at 3 a.m. with a racing heart and the Lord’s Prayer repeating like a hex in the back of their skull. This is the territory you walk alone, and that’s part of the repair: you, finally, as the sole authority over your internal weather. No intercessor. No mediator. Just your own hand on your own sternum, feeling the drumbeat that is yours, not His.

## FAQ: The Questions Nobody Lets You Ask

**Is it okay to be angry at God if I don’t even believe in God anymore?**
Absolutely. The anger is not a theological statement; it’s an attachment wound. The God-concept you were given functioned as the ultimate attachment figure—omnipotent, supposedly benevolent, and yet permissive of your harm. You can rage at a figure you’ve intellectually discarded because the emotional bond predated logic. It’s like being furious at a dead parent. The fact that they’re gone doesn’t dissolve the stored pain.

**What if my anger feels bottomless? I’m afraid I’ll never stop.**
The fear of endless anger is a leftover relic from a system that told you anger was sinful and must be suppressed. In truth, the body is designed to complete emotional cycles when they’re allowed to run their course. Peter Levine’s work shows that pent-up survival energy does not flow forever; it rises, peaks, and discharges—often within minutes—when you stop holding it back. The “bottomless” sensation is the result of damming, not of infinite volume.

**How can I reclaim my spirituality without betraying my rage?**
Start by separating spirituality from the institution that wounded you. Your rage is not anti-spiritual; it’s anti-oppression. Many people find that their anger clears the ground for a direct, unmediated experience of the sacred that has nothing to do with submission. On [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) there’s a companion piece about rebuilding a practice from the rubble that doesn’t ask you to forgive first or at all. Rage can be a form of devotion—a refusal to abandon the child who still waits for justice.

## The Nightbody and the Unwitnessed Scream

The hardest hours are between 2 and 4 a.m., when the neurochemical protections of daylight fade. You wake with a start, heart punching at your ribcage, a name on your lips that you didn’t choose. Maybe it’s a Bible verse you didn’t know you still memorized. Maybe it’s a single word like “foresaken.” The darkness of the room becomes indistinguishable from the felt absence of a protector you begged for as a child. This is the hour when religious trauma transforms into a full-body haunting.

Some find that low-dose glycine powder or a capsule—[NOW Glycine 1000 mg (100 veg capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00IVGC4EA?tag=spankyspinola-20) taken before bed can ease the nighttime startle by supporting inhibitory neurotransmitters that quiet the hypervigilant brain. But the deeper work is to stop fighting the wakefulness. Sit up in bed. Feel the sheets under your palms. Whisper to the child in the dark: *I am the one who came back for you. Not Him. Me.* Let the shiver run down your spine. That’s the body releasing a shard of frozen terror from a timeline that already ended. The body remembers the abandonment, but it also remembers the very first time someone—you—answered its call.

## What Heaven Might Look Like If It Could Take a Punch

Imagine a Heaven that isn’t a throne room, but a wide field of rust-colored grass. Your rage enters it as a physical thing—a storm, a creature, a hot wind—and nothing smites it. The sky, huge and indifferent, doesn’t part. There is no voice telling you to be still. No celestial father demanding reverence. Just space. Your fury rolls across that field, unjudged, and eventually tires itself out under a sun that doesn’t ask you to repent. The grass is flattened; tomorrow it will rise again.

This isn’t a promise of an afterlife. It’s a reclamation of the psychic real estate that was colonized by a deity who required your self-erasure. The anger at Heaven is, at its core, a demand that the cosmos account for your suffering. That accounting may never come in a form you recognize. But the act of demanding it—fully, bodily, with a voice that shakes the larynx and a spine that refuses to bend—reshapes your inner firmament. You become, perhaps for the first time, the authority over your own interior sky. And that is a kind of kingdom worth inhabiting.
